title: A Bidirectional Quantum-Electrodynamic Model of Consciousness: Testing the Consciousness Identity Factor via Anomalous Hodgkin-Huxley Waveform Deviations
author: SENARATH DAYATHILAKE, KANDE LEKAMALAYA
license_note: Zenodo record metadata license field: cc-by-4.0
description: This paper introduces a novel bidirectional Quantum-Electrodynamic (QED) model of consciousness centered on a non‑copyable Consciousness Identity Factor (CIF). The CIF is hypothesized to arise from ultra‑quantum genomic particles that couple bidirectionally with the brain’s macroscopic electromagnetic field. The author formally derives the interaction Lagrangian and the modified Liouville‑von Neumann equations, showing how the quantum density matrix can inject an anomalous current density into classical neural dynamics. A key falsifiable prediction is a 0.5–3.0 \,\text{mV} deviation from standard Hodgkin‑Huxley action potential waveforms during conscious states—distinct from thermal and channel noise. The paper presents a rigorous experimental protocol using dual somato‑dendritic patch‑clamp electrophysiology on human iPSC‑derived cortical neurons, combined with differential chemical ablation (propofol vs. xenon gas) to isolate the quantum‑biological interface. Signal processing via Kalman filtering and power spectral analysis is outlined to extract the predicted anomaly. The framework resolves long‑standing issues in consciousness studies: it avoids the dualistic interaction problem (energy conservation is maintained) and solves the multi‑brain replication paradox via the quantum no‑cloning theorem, thereby protecting the unicity of subjective identity within a non‑reductive physicalist approach.
